context: who is the image of the invisible God
point_summary: The phrase "image of the invisible God" in verse 15 is anchored grammatically to "his dear Son" in verse 13, establishing that the entire exalted description applies specifically to Jesus Christ.

context: He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of every creature
point_summary: The "who is" of verse 15 is grammatically anchored to "his dear Son" in verse 13, establishing that the image of the invisible God and firstborn of every creature is specifically the Lord Jesus Christ.

context: for by him were all things created that are in heaven and that are in earth, visible and invisible, whether they be thrones or dominions or principalities or powers, all things were created by him and for him
point_summary: All things in heaven and earth — visible and invisible, thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers — were created both by Christ and for Christ, establishing his absolute supremacy over all creation.

context: And he is before all things, and by him all things consist
point_summary: Christ's preexistence and sustaining role over all creation are presented as evidence of His exaltation, with verse 17 read as part of a cumulative portrait establishing His supreme identity.

context: And he is the head of the body, the church, who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, that in all things he might have the preeminence
point_summary: Christ's headship over the church and preeminence in all things is presented as the culminating descriptor in a series of titles establishing who the Son is.

context: for it pleased the Father that in him should all fullness dwell
point_summary: The Father's good pleasure in having all fullness dwell in Christ is presented as foundational to understanding Christ's preeminence over all things.

context: And having made peace through the blood of his cross, by him, to reconcile all things unto himself, by him I say, whether they be things in earth or things in heaven
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Number one, we find right here in v. 15 and really down through a number of verses, v. 19 in particular, but the first way in which He is described is that He is the image of the invisible God.
point_summary: Christ's designation as "the image of the invisible God" is expounded through the meaning of *image* as likeness and manifestation, establishing that Jesus is the very God in flesh.

context: just as Colossians 1 deals with the eternality of Christ, so also John 17 deals with the eternality of Christ.
point_summary: Christ's identity as the image of the invisible God in Colossians 1 is illuminated by John 1:14, 18 and John 17:5, which together confirm both His incarnational manifestation and His eternal pre-existence.

context: we see that over in the book of Colossians, stay here in John, I'm gonna read another couple of verses here, but in the book of Colossians in chapter number one, we see the eternality of Christ in verse number 17 when the Bible says He is before all things, and by Him all things consist.
point_summary: Christ's eternality—His existence before all creation—is established in Colossians 1:17 ("He is before all things") and confirmed by Jesus' own prayer in John 17:5 for the glory He shared with the Father before the world existed.

context: He is the image of the invisible God
point_summary: Christ's designation as "the image of the invisible God" in verse 15 is read alongside verse 19 to establish that all divine fullness dwells in Him alone, countering any distribution of deity among lesser beings.

context: for it pleased the Father
point_summary: Christ's full deity is affirmed in Colossians 1:19 by the declaration that all fullness dwells in Him, implicitly refuting any teaching that distributes divine attributes among angels or lesser beings.

context: the kingdom of His dear Son
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He is the image of the invisible God. Number two, He is the Creator. At the end of verse number 15, the Bible says the firstborn of every creature.
point_summary: Christ's designation as "firstborn of every creature" (v. 15) denotes not creaturely origin but supreme heirship and sovereignty over all creation, a meaning confirmed by comparing Scripture with Scripture.

context: He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of every creature
point_summary: "Firstborn of every creature" carries the Old Testament sense of supremacy and heirship, not chronological origin, refuting the claim that Christ was a created being rather than the Creator of all things.

context: For by Him were all things created, that are in heaven, and that are in earth, visible and invisible, whether they be thrones, or dominions, or principalities, or powers: all things were created by Him, and for Him
point_summary: "Firstborn of every creature" denotes Christ's supremacy and heirship over all creation, not His being created, since verse 16 declares all things were made by Him and for Him.

context: And He is before all things, and by Him all things consist
point_summary: Christ's preexistence and sustaining power are affirmed in verse 17—"He is before all things, and by Him all things consist"—complementing Genesis 1 rather than contradicting it.

context: for by Him, in verse number 16, were all things created
point_summary: Colossians 1:16's declaration that all things were created by Christ complements rather than contradicts Genesis 1, supplying revealed detail about Jesus as the specific agent of creation.

context: The Bible says in verse number 16, notice it says, all things were created by Him. It tells us that are in heaven and that are in earth, visible and invisible.
point_summary: Christ's creative work encompasses every category of being — visible and invisible, including angelic hierarchies of thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers — meaning angels themselves are created beings under His authority.

context: by him all things consist
point_summary: Christ's role as sustainer is inseparable from his role as creator — remove God, and the entire universe collapses, because by him all things consist.

context: he is the image of the invisible God
point_summary: Christ as "the image of the invisible God" (Col. 1:15) means he made the invisible God visible and knowable to mankind.

context: and having made peace through the blood of His cross by Him to reconcile all things unto Himself. By Him, I say, whether they be things on earth or things in heaven.
point_summary: Reconciliation with God comes exclusively through the blood of Christ's cross, the only basis on which sinners alienated by wicked works can be brought to peace with God.

context: and having made peace through the blood of His cross
point_summary: Peace with God is not something sinners achieve but something Christ accomplished through the blood of His cross, reconciling those who were once alienated enemies by wicked works.

context: to present you holy and unblameable and unreprovable in His sight
point_summary: Reconciliation through Christ's substitutionary death results in believers being presented holy, unblameable, and unreprovable before God—not in themselves, but clothed in Christ's imputed righteousness.

context: who hath delivered us from the power of darkness and hath translated us into the kingdom of His dear Son
point_summary: Salvation's transfer—deliverance from darkness and translation into Christ's kingdom—is the foundational act that makes possible the believer's final presentation as holy, unblameable, and unreprovable before God.

context: In Colossians 2, in verse number 10, is the verse of Scripture in which we find that it says, and you are complete in Him
point_summary: Because Christ's redemptive work clothes believers in His righteousness, Colossians 2:10's declaration of completeness in Him means nothing more is needed for God to present His children holy, unblameable, and unreprovable.

context: Whom we preach, warning every man and teaching every man in all wisdom that we may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus.
point_summary: Paul's preaching mission in Colossians 1:28 is driven by the goal of presenting every person perfect in Christ—the same unblameable standing before God described in verse 22.

context: and He is the head of the body, the church. Who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, that in all things He might have the preeminence.
point_summary: Christ's headship over the church, drawn from the word "head" in verse 18, identifies Him as the originator, source, and ruling authority — establishing that the church belongs to God, not man.

context: The Bible says this at the end of verse 18. That in all things, He might have the preeminence.
point_summary: Christ's supreme identity as image of God, Creator, Redeemer, and head of the church demands the logical response that He hold first place—preeminence—in every area of life.

context: Notice in verse number 23, The Bible says this, if you continue in the faith, grounded and settled, and be not moved away from the hope of the gospel, which ye have heard, and which was preached to every creature which is under heaven, whereof I, Paul, am made a minister.
point_summary: Continuing in the faith—grounded, settled, and unmoved from the gospel—is presented as the second essential component of the appropriate response to Christ's preeminence.

context: strengthened with all might, according to his glorious power, unto all patience and longsuffering, with joyfulness
point_summary: Strengthened with all might according to God's glorious power (v. 11), the divine enablement Paul prayed for produces the spiritual maturity, endurance, and steadfastness that keeps believers continuing in the faith.

context: if you continue in the faith, grounded and settled, and be not moved away from the hope of the gospel, which ye have heard, and which was preached to every creature which is under heaven, whereof I, Paul, am made a minister
point_summary: Continuing in the faith—grounded, settled, and unmoved from the gospel—is the appropriate response to Christ's lordship, guarding believers against the threat of false teachers.

context: if you continue in the faith, grounded and settled, and be not moved away.
point_summary: Continuing in the faith, grounded and settled, is not a condition for keeping salvation but a safeguard against the effectiveness-robbing confusion that false teachers introduce into the lives of already-secure believers.

context: the saints and faithful brethren in Christ, which are at Colossae.
point_summary: The designation of the Colossians as "saints and faithful brethren in Christ" in 1:2 establishes that Paul's warning in 1:23 addresses believers whose salvation is secure, not people at risk of losing it.

context: continue in prayer, and watch in the same with thanksgiving
point_summary: Paul's appeal to a church he had never personally met—urging them to pray for an open door to preach Christ—illustrates that intercessory prayer transcends personal acquaintance and reflects genuine belief in prayer's power.

context: that the Bible tells us in Colossians chapter number one, that He hath made us meet to be partakers of the inheritance of the saints in light. In verse number 12, that word meet means that He's made us qualified.
point_summary: Colossians 1:12's word "meet" is expounded to mean that God alone qualifies sinners for heaven, since no human good work can earn that standing.

context: blotting out the handwriting of ordinances that was against us, which was contrary to us, and took it out of the way, nailing it to His cross.
point_summary: Christ's sacrifice was fully sufficient—the debt of ordinances against us was cancelled and nailed to the cross, securing a victory at Calvary that defeated the devil himself.

context: And having spoiled principalities and powers, He made a show of them openly, triumphing over them in it.
point_summary: Christ's triumph at Calvary—openly spoiling principalities and powers—is presented as the decisive defeat of the devil, who mistakenly believed he had won.

context: And according to Colossians chapter two, God, the Lord Jesus Christ, spoiled principalities and powers, and He made a show of them openly.
point_summary: Christ's resurrection victory over sin and death is the basis for Colossians 2's declaration that He spoiled principalities and powers, making an open show of their defeat.

point_summary: Paul's address to "saints and faithful brethren in Christ" at Colossae establishes that his recipients were already saved, which is why his prayer seeks their growth rather than their conversion.

context: for the hope which is laid up for you in heaven, whereof ye heard before in the word of the truth of the gospel
point_summary: Paul's knowledge that the Colossians had already heard the gospel and were bearing fruit from it informed his prayer for their growth rather than their salvation.

context: which is come unto you, as it is in all the world, and bringeth forth fruit, notice, as it doth also in you, since the day ye heard of it, and knew the grace of God in truth
point_summary: The gospel's universal fruitfulness—bearing fruit "in all the world" and specifically among the Colossians—confirmed to Paul that these believers were genuinely saved, informing the nature of his prayer for them.

Now, any time you see a beware or a warning, I warn you in the word of God, we ought to take note to that, and we ought to kind of perk up our ears.
point_summary: Paul's warning in Colossians 2:8 against philosophy, vain deceit, and the traditions of men establishes the presence of active false teachers as the third piece of intelligence shaping Paul's intercessory prayer for the Colossians.

context: We see that at the end of verse number three in Colossians 1, and Paul is revealing to the believers in Colossae that he is committed to this ministry of intercessory prayer.
point_summary: Paul's phrase "praying always for you" in verse 3 is cited as evidence of his sustained, unwavering commitment to intercessory prayer on behalf of the Colossian believers.

context: Not only that, but I want you to take note in verse number nine, the Bible says, for
point_summary: Paul's unceasing intercession for the Colossians, stated in verse 9, is presented as evidence of his deep commitment to the ministry of intercessory prayer.

context: Paul's prayer for the Colossians we'll look at Colossians 1 verses 9 through 14
point_summary: Paul's unceasing intercession for the Colossians, shared jointly with Timothy, is presented as a model of committed, persistent prayer that should evaluate and shape the believer's own prayer life.

context: for this cause we also since the day we heard it do not cease to pray for you and to desire that you might be filled with the knowledge of his will in all wisdom and spiritual understanding
point_summary: Spiritual growth stands as the central emphasis of Paul's intercession in verse 9, where his unceasing prayer is that believers be filled with knowledge of God's will, wisdom, and spiritual understanding.

context: that you might walk worthy of the Lord and all-pleasing being fruitful in every good work and increasing in the knowledge of God
point_summary: Spiritual growth is identified as the central emphasis of Paul's prayer, with walking worthy, bearing fruit, and increasing in the knowledge of God marking the shape of that growth.

context: strengthened with all might according to his glorious power and all patience and long-suffering with joyfulness
point_summary: Spiritual growth is identified as the central emphasis of Paul's prayer in verses 9–11, with strengthening through God's glorious power serving the believers' ongoing walk, patience, and fruitfulness.

context: The Bible says this in verse number 10, that ye might walk worthy of the Lord unto all pleasing, being fruitful in every good work, and increasing in the knowledge of God.
point_summary: Knowing God's will (v. 9) must progress to doing God's will (v. 10), so that walking worthy of the Lord means active, obedient fruitfulness—not merely hearing but doing.

context: that ye might walk worthy of the Lord unto all pleasing, being fruitful in every good work
point_summary: Walking worthy of the Lord, according to this passage, produces fruit in every good work — and obedience to God's revealed will is the condition under which He grants increasing knowledge of Himself.

context: unto all patience and longsuffering
point_summary: Spiritual maturity requires both *patience* (endurance under difficult circumstances) and *longsuffering* (slowness to wrath toward people), two distinct qualities Paul prays God's power will produce in believers.

context: He hath made us meet to be partakers of the inheritance of the saints in light.
point_summary: Qualification for heaven is entirely God's work, not human effort—grounded in the imputed righteousness of Christ, by which believers are made "meet" to inherit the saints' eternal portion.

context: Who hath delivered us from the power of darkness.
point_summary: God's act of deliverance in Colossians 1:13 is set against the reality of a genuine "power of darkness" — Satan's blinding dominion over unbelievers — from which believers have been rescued.

context: he hath delivered us from the power of darkness... And hath translated us into the kingdom of his dear Son
point_summary: Salvation is framed as a decisive divine transaction — a deliverance out of Satan's real dominion of darkness and a transfer into the kingdom of God's Son.

context: We were delivered from the power of darkness and translated into the kingdom of his dear Son.
point_summary: Salvation is a supernatural transaction accomplished in a single moment, by which the believer is delivered from the power of darkness and translated into the kingdom of God's dear Son.

context: In whom we have redemption through his blood, even the forgiveness of sins.
point_summary: Redemption in Christ is not merely positional but transactional—accomplished through the blood of Christ at a definite moment, securing forgiveness of sins for all who believe.

context: Notice in Colossians 2, we're preaching through the book of Colossians on Sunday mornings. Our theme throughout this year has been found in verse number 10, that we are complete in Christ and what a blessed truth and thought that is. But we find, and I preached this passage of scripture many months ago, but notice what Paul says to these believers in Colossians 2 in verse number four. In this I say, lest any man should beguile you with enticing words.
point_summary: Beguiling through enticing words—false reasoning dressed in persuasive speech—is the same deceptive threat Paul warns against in Ephesians, confirming that believers must guard against deception in every form.

context: in this I say, lest any man should beguile you with enticing words.
point_summary: Deceivers do not announce their intent but use purposefully persuasive, enticing words — speech adapted to lead others into error through false reasoning — making beguiling indistinguishable from honest persuasion.

context: A few verses later in verse number eight, he'll say, beware, lest any man spoil you through philosophy and vain deceit after the tradition of men, after the rudiments of the world, and not after Christ.
point_summary: Deceivers operate through philosophically sophisticated but hollow arguments rooted in human tradition rather than Christ, making discernment against enticing, persuasive speech essential for believers.

context: ...we find here in Colossians 3 verses 1 through 3...
point_summary: Colossians 3:1 grounds the call to pursue Christ in the believer's secure position in Him — a position that, once understood, shapes what one pursues.

context: ...we find here in Colossians 3 verses 1 through 3...
point_summary: Colossians 3:2 is cited as part of a three-verse unit (vv. 1–3) calling believers to align their pursuits with their position in Christ, with the understanding that one's identity in Christ shapes what one seeks.

context: ...we find here in Colossians 3 verses 1 through 3...
point_summary: Colossians 3:3 grounds the believer's secure position in Christ, which the sermon presents as the foundation that shapes what Christians pursue in their daily lives.

context: when Christ who is our life shall appear then shall ye also appear with him in glory
point_summary: Colossians 3:4 is introduced as an eschatological verse containing a parenthetical statement—"Christ who is our life"—that warrants distinct attention before the passage's prophetic truth is fully unpacked.

context: When Christ, who is our life, notice in Colossians 1 and verse 18, and we studied this several months ago as we studied through the first chapter
point_summary: Christ's preeminence as head of the church and firstborn from the dead in Colossians 1:18 grounds the claim that He alone deserves the place of preeminence in the believer's life.

context: He is the head of the body of the church, who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, that in all things he might have the preeminence.
point_summary: Christ's designation as head of the church and firstborn from the dead grounds the claim that He must have preeminence in all things—not some, not most—in the life of every born-again believer.

context: Paul's writing to the saints and faithful brethren in Christ which are at Colossae
point_summary: Paul's address to the "saints and faithful brethren in Christ" at Colossae (1:2) establishes that the call for Christ's preeminence in all things is directed specifically to believers, not the unregenerate.

context: I'm so thankful this morning for this distinct perspective that is shared with us in Colossians 3 and verse 4. It's a parenthetical statement, but oh, how impactful it is.
point_summary: Christ being called "our life" in Colossians 3:4 is a parenthetical yet profoundly impactful declaration that He is the believer's all in all, without whom nothing can be accomplished.

context: when Christ, who is our life, shall appear, then shall ye also appear with him in glory.
point_summary: The word "shall" in Colossians 3:4 is treated as a declaration of certainty — Christ's appearing is not a possibility but a guaranteed promise from a God who cannot lie and has never failed.

context: ...this event that's mentioned here in Colossians 3 and verse number 4 really is dealing with the truth...
point_summary: Colossians 3:4's word "appear" (meaning to become manifest, to make actual and visible) identifies this event as the second coming of Christ—Revelation 19—not the rapture of 1 Thessalonians 4.

context: when we look at Colossians 3 and verse number 4, notice the word when the Bible says this, when Christ shall, notice this word appear. When Christ shall appear.
point_summary: Colossians 3:4's "appear" (meaning to become manifest, to make visible) is linked to Revelation 19's second advent, not the Rapture of 1 Thessalonians 4, and the "ye" who appear with Christ in glory extends to all believers.

context: when Christ shall appear, it says this, then shall ye also appear with him in glory
point_summary: Christ's appearing in Colossians 3:4 is distinguished from the rapture—where believers are caught up suddenly—and identified as a visible, manifest return in glory that includes all believers, not only those in Colossae.

context: when Christ, who is our life, shall appear, then shall ye also appear with him in glory
point_summary: Colossians 3:4's "shall appear" is distinguished from the rapture and applied specifically to the second coming, when believers visibly return with Christ all the way to earth.

context: he shall appear, then shall ye also appear with him in glory
point_summary: Christ's appearing in glory (Col. 3:4) and his return as conquering King (Rev. 19) are two distinct events separated by seven years, not interchangeable moments of his second coming.

context: when Christ who is our life shall appear, then shall ye also appear with him in glory
point_summary: The "appearing with Christ in glory" in Colossians 3:4 is identified as the second advent of Revelation 19, distinct from the Rapture, with the Tribulation falling between those two heavenly openings.

context: when Christ who is our life shall appear, then shall ye also appear with him in glory
point_summary: The promise that believers shall appear with Christ in glory motivates present-day living beyond earthly accumulation, offering confidence that this life is not all there is.

context: Colossians 2, verses 4 through 7. The Bible says this, And this I say, lest any man should beguile you with enticing words.
point_summary: Paul's warning against being "beguiled with enticing words" frames spiritual growth through the Word as the believer's most effective defense against false teaching.

context: Colossians 2, verses 4 through 7. The Bible says this, For though I be absent in the flesh, yet am I with you in the Spirit, joying and beholding your order, and the steadfastness of your faith in Christ.
point_summary: Spiritual growth is the most effective defense against false teaching, so that grounded believers see false philosophies fade under the truth of God's Word.
Paul's warning against being "beguiled with enticing words" identifies false teaching as deception through specious, persuasive reasoning specifically designed to lead believers into error.

context: Can you see the contrast between what Paul said to the believers in Colossae in chapter 1?
point_summary: Paul's goal in Colossians 1—to present every man perfect in Christ Jesus—stands in direct contrast to the false teachers' aim to beguile and deceive the believers.

context: For though I be absent in the flesh
point_summary: Paul's commendation of the Colossians in verse 5 rests on his spiritual nearness to a congregation he had never physically met, rejoicing over their order despite the distance separating them.

context: Epaphras was the spiritual leader at the church at Colossae
point_summary: Epaphras served as the informational link between Paul and the Colossian church, bringing back the report through which Paul came to know and rejoice over their condition despite never meeting them in person.

context: he says this at the end of verse number 5, and the steadfastness of your faith in Christ
point_summary: Paul's joy over the Colossians, even from prison, rests on two military-tinged realities: their orderly, disciplined unity and the firmness of a faith whose worth depends entirely on its object—Christ himself.

context: And so how does Paul challenge these believers in Colossae? How is it that Colossians 2 would challenge me, would challenge you this morning in verses 6 and 7?
point_summary: Paul's commendation of the Colossians' order and faith becomes the launching point for a challenge against spiritual apathy and stagnation, pressing believers to continue advancing in their walk rather than settling where they are.

context: As ye have therefore received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk ye in Him.
point_summary: Walking in Christ must follow the same path by which one received Him—faith alone—just as the Galatians' error illustrated the danger of beginning in faith but continuing in the flesh.

context: Rooted and built up in Him. We've said in Christ.
point_summary: Being "built up" in Christ describes a constant, progressive increase in Christian knowledge through continued engagement with Scripture, not through seeking new or outside teaching.

context: He says in verse number seven, as ye also learned of Epaphras, our dear fellow servant, who is for you a faithful minister of Christ.
point_summary: Faithful teaching from a trustworthy minister—exemplified by Epaphras—is the established foundation believers are called to remain in rather than seek other sources.

context: Colossians chapter number two, notice what he says. Think about this truth, when he says, as you have been taught, and then he says this, abounding therein.
point_summary: Abounding in Christ, as Colossians 2 commands, means not seeking new revelation but overflowing with thanksgiving for the complete, unchanging truth already delivered in Scripture.

context: he says this in Colossians 2. He says, with thanksgiving, abounding therein with thanksgiving.
point_summary: Abounding in Christ "with thanksgiving" calls believers to active, overflowing gratitude—specifically for the privilege of possessing God's Word and those who faithfully preach it.

context: It's referred to in verse 25 as the dispensation of God, which is given to me for you.
point_summary: Paul's stewardship ("the dispensation of God given to me for you") is cited to show his God-assigned responsibility to finish his ministry calling faithfully, contrasting his philosophy with that of false teachers.

context: Christ in you, the hope of glory
point_summary: Christ in you, the hope of glory (Col. 1:27) anchors Paul's three-part ministry philosophy—preaching Christ, warning every man, and teaching every man—as the contextual foundation for what follows in chapter two.

context: you find in verse number eight that he uses the word, beware
point_summary: The word "beware" in Colossians 2:8 is linked to Paul's ministry pattern of warning every man, establishing a direct continuity between his chapter-one framework and his alert against false philosophy.

context: for I would that ye knew what great conflict I have for you and for them at Laodicea, and for as many as have not seen my face in the flesh
point_summary: Paul's self-sacrificial concern for believers he had never met is so intense that he describes it as a "great conflict," contrasting sharply with the self-serving motives of false teachers.

context: that their hearts might be comforted, being knit together in love, and unto all riches of the full assurance of understanding, to the acknowledgement of the mystery of God and of the Father and of Christ
point_summary: Paul's deep concern for believers he had never met is described as a "great conflict," with the goal in verse 2 being their hearts comforted, knit together in love, and brought to full assurance of understanding.

context: in whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge
point_summary: In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge — the goal toward which Paul's agonizing concern for these believers is ultimately directed.

context: as I see the heart of the Apostle Paul in Colossians chapter number 2, in saying to these believers in Colossae that I would that ye knew what great conflict I have for you
point_summary: Paul's declaration of "great conflict" for believers he had never met is interpreted through 2 Corinthians 11:28 as the daily burden of caring for all the churches.

context: As we continue and move through the passage of Scripture here in Colossians 2, we consider, number two, the reason for this conflict.
point_summary: Paul's declaration of "great conflict" for believers he had never met reveals the weight of pastoral care as a burden carried for entire churches, not merely individuals known personally.

context: to whom God would make known what is the riches of the glory of this mystery among the Gentiles, which is Christ in you, the hope of glory.
point_summary: Against Gnostic claims to exclusive spiritual knowledge, Paul counters in 1:27 that the true mystery is Christ in you—a glory made known openly to all Gentiles, not hidden from them.

context: in whom, speaking of Christ, are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.
point_summary: Against the Gnostics' claim to exclusive wisdom and hidden knowledge, Paul counters that all treasures of wisdom and knowledge are already fully hidden in Christ alone.

context: ...but those that are in Laodicea, and as many as have not seen my face...
point_summary: Paul's concern in Colossians 2 extends beyond Colossae to Laodicea, the same church later addressed in Revelation 3, connecting his apostolic burden to a congregation he had never personally visited.

context: we look at verses 2 and 3 this morning of Colossians chapter number 2
point_summary: Paul's pastoral conflict finds its resolution in the knitting together of believers' hearts and their full assurance of understanding, as the antidote to the deception of false teachers who corrupt familiar vocabulary.

context: we look at verses 2 and 3 this morning of Colossians chapter number 2
point_summary: In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge—a reality Paul presents as the sufficient answer to false teaching that misappropriates Christian vocabulary while meaning something entirely different.

context: Notice what Paul says back in Colossians 2 in verse number 2. As he's expressing his desire for these believers that I believe would lead to the resolving of this conflict that he was dealing with, it says that their hearts, number 1 in verse number 2 there, that their hearts might be comforted.
point_summary: Paul's desire that the Colossians' hearts "might be comforted" addresses the anxiety and confusion caused by false teachers, with the word carrying the sense of encouragement and strengthening through consolation.

context: in chapter two and verse number two, being knit together in love.
point_summary: The phrase "knit together in love" in Colossians 2:2 is read as a picture of unity produced when believers collectively embrace the truth of God's Word.

context: ...and unto all riches of the full assurance of understanding to the acknowledgement of the mystery of God unto the Father and of Christ...
point_summary: Unity among believers is grounded in being "knit together in love," which leads to the full assurance of understanding found in Scripture alone, not in extra revelation from false teachers.

context: ...in whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.
point_summary: All treasures of wisdom and knowledge being hidden in Christ means Scripture's completed canon provides full assurance of understanding, leaving no room for extra-biblical revelation from false teachers.

context: The Scriptures tell us in the book of Colossians, we find that here he tells the believers in Colossians 1, the believers in Colossae, he says that in all things, he, Christ, the head of the church, might have the preeminence.
point_summary: Proving what is acceptable to the Lord requires giving Christ preeminence in all things—behavior, speech, entertainment, and dress—rather than measuring acceptability by cultural standards.

context: When we think about Christ in you, the hope of glory.
point_summary: Christ in you, the hope of glory (Col. 1:27) grounds the believer's readiness to give an answer, because this hope is not wishful thinking but a certain, indwelling reality the world does not possess.

context: if you then be risen with Christ, seek those things which are above, where Christ sitteth on the right hand of God.
point_summary: Genuine revival requires not merely filling churches with people but individuals being filled with God, a desperation Paul frames as setting affection on things above where Christ reigns.

context: Set your affection on things above, not on things of the earth.
point_summary: Genuine revival requires setting affection on things above—seeking God himself, not merely his blessings or church-growth results—because the kingdom advances through individuals filled with God, not just filled buildings.

context: For you're dead and your life is hid with Christ in God.
point_summary: Spiritual desperation to seek God himself—not merely his blessings—is grounded in the believer's identity as one who is dead and whose life is hidden with Christ, compelling affections set wholly on things above.

context: ye are Complete in him which is the head of all principality and Power
point_summary: Believers are declared complete in Christ, who is head of all principality and power — a truth drawn from Colossians 2:10 as the governing theme for the year's study through Colossians.

context: for though I be absent in the flesh Yet am I with you in the Spirit joining and beholding your order and the steadfastness notice of your faith in Christ
point_summary: Paul's rejoicing over the Colossians' orderly faith and steadfastness establishes that completeness in Christ is the controlling theme threading through the surrounding passage, anchoring the sermon's 2021 focus.

context: as Ye have therefore received Christ Jesus the Lord So walk ye in him
point_summary: Walking in Christ Jesus the Lord (v. 6) is the pattern of life that flows from having received Him, grounding the sermon's theme that completeness in Christ is both a standing and a daily walk.

context: rooted and built up in him who's him Jesus Christ and Established in the faith as ye have been taught abounding therein with thanksgiving
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Beware lest any man spoil you through philosophy and vain deceit After their tradition of men after the rudiments of the world and not after Christ
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The word complete in Colossians two in verse number 10, the verse that we're using as our theme verse for this year, if you turn back to that portion of Scripture of Colossians, the word complete means to fill to the full,
point_summary: Being complete in Christ begins with new birth, since the Greek word for "complete" in Colossians 2:10 means to fill to the full, render perfect in every particular—a fullness impossible for those still dead in trespasses and sins.

context: Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, and Timotheus, our brother.
point_summary: Paul's identification of the Colossian recipients as "saints and faithful brethren in Christ" establishes that they were born-again believers, which is the foundation for his declaration that they are complete in Him.

context: To the saints and faithful brethren in Christ, which are at Colossae.
point_summary: Paul's address to the Colossians as "saints and faithful brethren in Christ" establishes that his declaration of their completeness in Christ rests on the fact that they had been born again and were genuinely in Him.

context: since we heard of your faith in Christ Jesus, and of the love which you have to all the saints
point_summary: The mention of faith in Christ Jesus in Colossians 1:4 is cited as evidence that the Colossian recipients were born-again believers, establishing why Paul's declaration of completeness in Christ applies to them.

context: the steadfastness of your faith in Christ
point_summary: The "steadfastness of your faith in Christ" in Colossians 2:5 is cited as evidence that the Colossian recipients were genuine believers, establishing why Paul could declare them complete in Him.

context: to them in Colossians 2 and verse number 10, and ye are complete in him.
point_summary: Completeness in Christ belongs exclusively to those who have been born again, saved by grace through faith — the very audience Paul addresses throughout Colossians.

context: look specifically at this truth this morning, from this thought in Colossians 2, that I am complete in Him, in Christ.
point_summary: Completeness in Christ is received at the moment of new birth — the point when a sinner places faith in the finished work of Christ at Calvary and is made alive.

context: for I would that ye knew what great conflict I have for you, and for them at Laodicea, and for as many as have not seen my face in the flesh.
point_summary: Paul's deep personal conflict and intercessory burden for believers he had never met personally frames the pastoral urgency underlying his teaching on completeness in Christ.

context: as we've mentioned already in verse number six, notice in Colossians 2 in verse 6, as ye have therefore received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk ye in him.
point_summary: Having received Christ Jesus the Lord, believers are called to walk in Him—a principle the sermon connects to the Christian life being rooted, built up, and established in faith.

context: beware, lest any man spoil you through philosophy and vain deceit. After the tradition of men, after the rudiments of the world, and not after Christ.
point_summary: Paul's warning "beware" in verse 8 functions as a danger sign alerting believers that philosophy, vain deceit, and human tradition pose a real threat to spoil those who are rooted and built up in Christ.

context: Beware, lest any man spoil you through these processes.
point_summary: Colossians 2:8 functions as a divine warning against philosophy, vain deceit, and human tradition—dangers that make the corresponding assurance of completeness in Christ (vv. 9–10) all the more necessary.

context: for in Him dwelleth all the fullness of the Godhead bodily.
point_summary: Because all the fullness of the Godhead dwells bodily in Christ, believers need look nowhere else — no philosophy, tradition, or deceit can add to what is already complete in Him.

context: ye are complete in Him, which is the head of all principality and power.
point_summary: Against the false philosophies and vain deceit warned of in v. 8, believers find their answer in v. 10: completeness in Christ alone renders every competing system unnecessary.

context: what great truth we find in Ephesians and Colossians with regard to doing that
point_summary: Ephesians and Colossians together supply the "put off the old man, put on the new" framework for understanding how believers must actively resist the flesh that can revive in an instant.

context: The Bible says in Colossians 1 that in all things He, Christ, must have the preeminence.
point_summary: Christ's preeminence over all things in Colossians 1:18 is invoked as the standard against which idolatry—whether ancient statues or modern pursuits like work, money, and status—is measured and condemned.

context: Children are commanded to obey their parents. And in Colossians 3.20, it says that in so doing, they are pleasing the Lord. So it's a simple verse of Scripture, a simple principle that we find again as we're studying and proving what is acceptable unto the Lord, that we find that God says in Colossians 3.20, children obey your parents and all things for this is well pleasing unto the Lord.
point_summary: Children's obedience to parents is presented as a concrete, scriptural example of pleasing the Lord, with disobedience framed as an offense against God, not merely against parents.

context: We looked at verses 11 through 15 and we dealt with that statement that he made in verse number 14, nailing it to his cross.
point_summary: The cancellation of the written record of debt against believers — "nailing it to his cross" — is recalled as the accomplished work of Christ at Calvary that grounds the believer's assurance of salvation.

context: Remember, as he mentioned to these believers in the first chapter in verse number 28 of chapter number one of the book of Colossians, he says, whom we preach, speaking of the Lord Jesus Christ, warning every man and teaching every man in all wisdom. Why? That we may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus.
point_summary: Paul's stated purpose in preaching—warning and teaching every man in all wisdom—is cited as the apostolic foundation for why believers must be guarded against the deceptive methods of false teachers.

context: The Bible says, let no man therefore judge you in meat or in drink or in respect of an holy day
point_summary: Legalistic judgment over food, drink, and sacred days is identified as a primary trap of false teaching, with such observances described as shadows now fulfilled in Christ.

context: Let no man therefore judge you in meat, or in drink, or in respect of an holyday, or of the new moon, or of the sabbath days
point_summary: Colossians 2:16's prohibition against judgment over food, drink, and holy days identifies legalism as the trap of depending on moral law and outward works rather than personal faith in Christ.

context: Which are a shadow of things to come; but the body is of Christ.
point_summary: Legalism—adding works to grace—is the trap Paul warns against in verses 16–17, where dependence on moral law replaces personal faith in Christ.

context: Let no man beguile you of your reward in a voluntary humility and worshipping of angels, intruding into those things which he hath not seen, vainly puffed up by his fleshly mind
point_summary: Verse 18's warning against being "beguiled" by voluntary humility and angel-worship is identified as a distinct second trap of false teaching, separate from the legalism addressed in verses 16–17.

context: And not holding the Head, from which all the body by joints and bands having nourishment ministered, and knit together, increaseth with the increase of God.
point_summary: Failing to hold fast to Christ as the Head — the source from whom the whole body receives nourishment and growth — is identified as the core error underlying false teaching's various traps.

context: Wherefore if ye be dead with Christ from the rudiments of the world, why, as though living in the world, are ye subject to ordinances
point_summary: Death with Christ from the world's elemental principles exposes legalism's core error: subjecting believers to man-made ordinances contradicts their union with Christ, who has already freed them from such systems.

context: Touch not; taste not; handle not;
point_summary: Colossians 2:21's prohibitions ("touch not, taste not, handle not") are presented as the defining language of legalism—a system that adds works to grace rather than resting in personal faith in Christ.

context: Which all are to perish with the using;) after the commandments and doctrines of men?
point_summary: Human regulations and commands — described in verse 22 as things that "perish with the using" — are identified here as the doctrinal foundation of the trap of legalism.

context: Which things have indeed a shew of wisdom in will worship, and humility, and neglecting of the body; not in any honour to the satisfying of the flesh.
point_summary: Verse 23's warning that human-devised religious strictness carries only an appearance of wisdom is set within a broader indictment of legalism—rules that add works to grace rather than resting in Christ.

context: in meat or in drink or in respect of a holy day or of the new moon or of the Sabbath days
point_summary: The legalists' judgment over food, drink, and holy days in Colossians 2:16 is identified as Old Testament shadow-keeping that Christ has already fulfilled, making such demands a mark of false teaching.

context: It's mentioned in verse number 16 of Colossians chapter number 2
point_summary: Colossians 2:16's reference to holy days, new moons, and Sabbaths is read against the transitional New Testament period in which Jewish believers and Gentiles were being united as one body, making such judgments a mark of false teaching.

context: And not holding the Head, from which all the body by joints and bands having nourishment ministered, and knit together, increaseth with the increase of God.
point_summary: Mysticism's fatal error, per verse 19, is failing to hold Christ as the Head — displacing Him from His proper position as the one through whom the whole body grows.

context: And he is the head of the body, the church: who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead; that in all things he might have the preeminence.
point_summary: Christ's supremacy as head of the church, established in Colossians 1, is the doctrinal foundation the mystics violated by failing to hold Christ in His proper position.

context: who is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of every creature
point_summary: Christ's identity as "the image of the invisible God" and Creator of all things establishes His preeminence as Head, the position mystics forfeit when they pursue spiritual experience outside of Him.

context: For by Him were all things created that are in heaven, that are in earth, visible and invisible, whether they be thrones or dominions or principalities or powers. All things were created by Him and for Him
point_summary: All things — visible, invisible, thrones, dominions, principalities, powers — were created by and for Christ, establishing His preeminence as the head whom mystics fail to hold properly.

context: And He, Christ, is before all things and by Him all things consist
point_summary: Christ's preeminence as the one before all things and by whom all things consist is the doctrinal ground for why displacing Him from His proper headship—whether in the church, the home, or mystical speculation—causes everything to go wrong.

context: And He is the head of the body, the church, who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, that in all things, He might have the preeminence
point_summary: Christ's headship over the church, declared in Colossians 1:18, is the standard against which mysticism fails—displacing Him from His proper position disorders both church and personal life.

context: Wherefore, if ye be dead with Christ from the rudiments of the world, why, as though living in the world, are you subject to ordinances?
point_summary: Failing to understand one's position as dead with Christ from the world's elemental principles leads to renewed enslavement under ordinances that Christ's cross has already rendered obsolete.

context: let no man therefore judge you
point_summary: Asceticism's trap is rooted in a failure to grasp one's position in Christ, which Paul counters in verse 16 with the command "let no man judge you," guarding believers' freedom.

context: let no man beguile you
point_summary: Failing to grasp one's position in Christ—being dead with Christ and set free—is what leaves believers vulnerable to the enslaving trap described in verse 18.

context: the text that we studied last week in verses 11 through 15
point_summary: Misunderstanding one's position in Christ—being dead with Him and freed from the world's rudiments—is identified as the root failure that leaves believers vulnerable to the bondage of asceticism.

context: the text that we studied last week in verses 11 through 15
point_summary: Failure to grasp one's position as dead with Christ—established in the cross-work of Colossians 2:11–15—is identified as the root cause of falling into the bondage of ascetic ordinances.

context: it was back in verse number eight of Colossians 2 that Paul says
point_summary: Colossians 2:8's warning against being spoiled through "the rudiments of the world" is recalled here as the very bondage from which believers have died in Christ, making subjection to worldly ordinances a contradiction of their position.

context: beware lest any man spoil you through philosophy and vain deceit after the tradition of men, after the rudiments of the world, and not after Christ.
point_summary: Colossians 2:8's warning against "the rudiments of the world" is cited as the anchor for understanding why believers who have died with Christ have no reason to remain subject to worldly ordinances.

context: if you're dead with Christ from the rudiments of the world, why, as though living in the world, are you subject to these ordinances?
point_summary: Dying with Christ severs believers from the world's rudiments, making continued submission to ordinances a failure to reckon with the position they already hold in him.

context: blotting out the handwriting of ordinances that was against us.
point_summary: The ordinances of the law stood as a written record of condemnation against every sinner, a guilt no one can escape—which makes Christ's blotting them out in verse 14 the only ground for freedom from legal bondage.

context: He blotted out the handwriting of ordinances that was against us, which was contrary to us, and he took it out of the way, nailing it to his cross.
point_summary: The handwriting of ordinances in Colossians 2:14 represents the condemning weight of the law—under which every person stands guilty—that Christ nailed to the cross, canceling its hold on believers.

context: Touch not, taste not, handle not
point_summary: The ordinances of "touch not, taste not, handle not" in verse 21 are identified as man-made commandments, not divine law, exposing ascetic religion as a human trap rather than God's requirement.

context: which all are to perish with the using. Notice the end of verse number 22. After the commandments and doctrines of who? Men.
point_summary: Human regulations that forbid touching, tasting, and handling are exposed as commandments of men, not of God—man-made additions that perish with use and carry no divine authority.

context: the Bible teaches us in the book of Ephesians and again in the book of Colossians that we're to put off and then put on
point_summary: Persistent temptation requires the ongoing discipline of "putting off" the old man and "putting on" the new, a principle the sermon draws from both Ephesians and Colossians 3:9 together.

context: Colossians 4 and verse number 6 says, let your speech be always with grace, seasoned with salt that you may know how you ought to answer every man.
point_summary: Colossians 4:6's command that speech be "always with grace, seasoned with salt" grounds the call to imitate Joseph's kindness by speaking graciously and compassionately to others.

context: In whom we have redemption through His blood, even the forgiveness of sins.
point_summary: Redemption through Christ's blood, as stated in Colossians 1:14, is presented as the unifying truth that connects the saving purpose of Jesus' name in Matthew 1:21 with the redemptive mission declared in Galatians 4:4–5.

context: He prays for the believers in Colossae in Colossians chapter number one
point_summary: Paul's intercession for the Colossians—believers he had never personally met—illustrates that God's will extends beyond salvation to the spiritual growth, wisdom, and fruitful walk of those already saved.

context: in Colossians 1, how does Paul pray for these believers in Colossae? Well, in verse number 9, it says, for this cause we also, notice what he says, since the day we heard it, do not cease to pray for you, and to desire that ye might be filled with the knowledge of his will and all wisdom and spiritual understanding
point_summary: Paul's intercessory prayer for believers he had never met in Colossae illustrates that God's will extends beyond salvation to the spiritual growth of those already saved.

context: Continue in prayer. Verse number 2 of Colossians 4. And watch in the same with thanksgiving with all.
point_summary: Paul's own prayer request in Colossians 4:2 — asking believers to pray that God would open a door of utterance to speak the mystery of Christ — parallels his request in Ephesians 6, showing his consistent dependence on others' intercession for gospel opportunity.

context: Paul says of a group of people that he's never met personally but he's seeking to minister to and he has a heart for them and he says to them in Colossians 1 and verse 28, as he kind of outlines and summarizes his... ministry. He says, whom we preach, warning every man and teaching every man in all wisdom, that we may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus.
point_summary: Faithful gospel ministry—warning and teaching every person in wisdom—is oriented toward the final goal of presenting every believer perfect in Christ before His judgment seat.

context: When paul said to these that he's writing to in colossi and ye are complete In him complete in christ complete in him
point_summary: Completeness in Christ serves as the thematic anchor for the entire series, introduced from Colossians 2:10 before the verse-by-verse study of chapter 1 begins.

context: the bible says this in verse number one of colossians 1 paul An apostle of jesus christ by the will of god Timotheus our brother
point_summary: Paul's identification as "an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God" is expounded here as his recognition that his calling, authority, and responsibility were entirely God's sovereign doing, not his own.

context: The bible says in verse number two of colossians one to The saints and faithful brethren in christ which are at colossi Grace be unto you and peace from god our father and The lord jesus christ
point_summary: Grace and peace are addressed specifically to those identified as "saints and faithful brethren in Christ," grounding the letter's recipients in their dual identity as set-apart and believing members of the body at Colosse.

context: if you read verse number one of chapter two the book of colossians The bible says for I would that ye knew what great conflict I have for you and for them At laodicea
point_summary: Paul's never having personally visited or met the Colossian believers, confirmed by his own words in 2:1, underscores that his deep love and concern for them was rooted in their shared faith, not personal acquaintance.

context: Paul's writing to them in verse 1
point_summary: Paul's authorship of Colossians is the basis for the original recipients' excitement, as a letter from the Apostle carried the weight of God's Word demanding immediate, life-impacting engagement.

context: the description in verse 2. The Bible says, to the saints
point_summary: The designation "saints" in Colossians 1:2 is treated as the defining description of these believers, with the sermon drawing attention to the term as a significant and thankworthy title for the recipients of Paul's letter.

context: to the saints and faithful brethren in Christ which are at Colossae
point_summary: The biblical term "saint" denotes not a post-mortem honor but every born-again believer—one who is in Christ and in whom Christ dwells—as Paul's consistent address to living congregations confirms.

context: Paul says, grace be unto you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: The Colossian believers' designation as "faithful brethren" is held up as a commendable quality, grounded in the biblical principle that faithfulness is required of God's stewards.

context: Notice in v. 3 of Colossians 1, we give thanks to God and the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ praying always for you.
point_summary: Paul's thanksgiving and intercession for the Colossian believers in 1:3 is noted here as a theme to be developed in a future message, with the verse serving as a bridge to his specific prayer in verses 9–14.

context: Notice in v. 4, since we heard of your faith in Christ Jesus and of the love which ye have to all the saints.
point_summary: Paul's thanksgiving for the Colossians in verse 4 is grounded in his having *heard* of their faith in Christ Jesus — their conversion, their new birth — which reached him by report rather than personal witness.

context: In v. 9-14, he lets them know how he's praying for them specifically.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for the hope which is laid up for you in Heaven, whereof ye heard before in the word of the truth of the Gospel
point_summary: The hope laid up in Heaven, mentioned in v. 5, is tied directly to the Colossians' reception of "the word of the truth of the Gospel," which came to them and spread throughout all the world.

context: which is come unto you as it is in all the world, and bringeth forth fruit as it doth also in you, since the day ye heard of it and knew the grace of God in truth
point_summary: The Gospel's universal reach—coming to Colosse as it spread throughout all the world—is presented as evidence of the Word of Truth's unstoppable, fruit-bearing advance among New Testament believers.

context: Notice where it says in v. 6, right in the middle of the verse, '...bringeth forth fruit.'
point_summary: The Gospel's universal reach and inherent fruitfulness—grounded in its power as God's means of salvation—demand that believers scatter it into the world rather than withhold it.

context: But I'm encouraged as I read verse 6 as the Bible tells me, listen, it doth also in you, it brought forth fruit in me, but it's gone not only to me, it's gone into all the world. And it bringeth forth fruit.
point_summary: The universal fruitfulness of the Gospel—demonstrated in the Colossians' own conversion—grounds confidence that faithful proclamation will bear fruit in others, wherever it goes.

context: who also declared unto us your love in the Spirit
point_summary: Epaphras served as the human messenger who carried news of the Colossians' love in the Spirit back to Paul, linking the local church to the apostle through faithful reporting.

context: since we heard of your faith in Christ Jesus, and of the love which ye have to all the saints
point_summary: The Colossian believers' love toward all the saints, reported to Paul by Epaphras, is presented as visible, verifiable evidence of genuine Christian community—faith expressed outwardly in love.

context: who also declared unto us your love in the Spirit
point_summary: Epaphras's report to Paul that the Colossian believers' love extended to "all the saints" serves as evidence that genuine gospel fruit is displayed in visible, community-wide love.

context: but I notice in these first few verses of Colossians 1,
point_summary: The love Colossian believers showed "to all the saints" is held up as a universal, undiscriminating standard that every congregation should visibly embody, with no exceptions for personality clashes.

context: A faithful minister of Christ. But we're noticing how they had been discipled.
point_summary: Epaphras's designation as "a faithful minister of Christ" in verse 7 sets the standard every pastor should aspire to meet when the Chief Shepherd returns.

context: In Colossians chapter 2, in verse number 6, the Bible says that the devil's purpose is this, is he have therefore received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk ye in him.
point_summary: Receiving Christ as Lord is the foundation for a walk that is rooted, built up, and established in the faith — in direct contrast to the philosophy and vain deceit warned against in the following verse.

context: Colossians 3 and verse 23 says this,
point_summary: Work done for God—whether simple or significant—deserves wholehearted effort because the believer's ultimate employer is the Lord, not men.

context: whatsoever you do, do it heartily as to the Lord and not unto men
point_summary: Whatever task God assigns—even cleaning bathrooms—is to be done with wholehearted effort because the Christian's true employer is the Lord, not men.

context: if you continue in the faith, grounded and settled, and be not moved away from the hope of the Gospel which ye have heard and which was preached to every creature which is under heaven, whereof I, Paul, am made a minister.
point_summary: Colossians 1:23's language of being "grounded and settled" is used to illustrate the strength and stability pictured in the tree planted by rivers of water in Psalm 1:3.

context: all my states shall Tychicus declare unto you, who is a beloved brother, and a faithful minister and fellowservant in the Lord
point_summary: Paul's commendation of Tychicus in Colossians 4:7 serves as the entry point into a closing section of personal greetings, where named individuals are described by their faithfulness and labor—setting up a contrast with the specific instruction later directed to Archippus in verse 17.

context: With Onesimus, a faithful and beloved brother, who is one of you. They shall make known unto you all things which are done here.
point_summary: Onesimus is identified in the closing greetings of Colossians as "a faithful and beloved brother," illustrating how Paul commends co-laborers by name as part of his broader pattern of affirming those engaged in ministry work.

context: And say to Archippus, Take heed to the ministry which thou hast received in the Lord, that thou fulfil it.
point_summary: Paul's singling out of Archippus in Colossians 4:17 with a specific charge—distinct from the favorable descriptions given to other co-laborers—signals that personal, direct instruction to take heed to one's ministry was urgently needed for this fellow soldier.

context: ...the only two verses of Scripture that we have specifically about this man archivist or the two that we've looked at this evening Philemon one verse number two and here in Colossians 4 and verse number 17...
point_summary: Archippus's call to "fulfill" the ministry he received in the Lord underscores that God-given ministry carries an obligation of completion, not merely commencement.

context: And say to Archipis, Take heed to the ministry which thou hast received in the Lord, that thou fulfill it.
point_summary: Colossians 4:17's charge to Archippus—"take heed to the ministry…that thou fulfill it"—is read as Paul's call to persevering faithfulness, urging a fellow soldier not to abandon his God-given ministry under pressure.

context: Take heed to the ministry which thou hast received in the Lord, that thou fulfill it.
point_summary: Archippus's charge to "fulfill" the ministry he received stands as a call to persevere in one's God-given assignment rather than surrender to discouragement at what feels like a dead end.

context: and whatsoever you do, do it heartily as to the Lord and not unto men
point_summary: Colossians 3:23 grounds Joseph's diligent labor as worship, establishing that believers' work ethic should be visibly distinct from the world's because it is rendered unto God, not men.

context: Whom we preach, warning every man and teaching every man in all wisdom that we may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus.
point_summary: Paul's pastoral goal—presenting every believer complete in Christ—is cited to explain why he invested so deeply in churches he had never personally visited, driven by love rather than personal acquaintance.

context: He speaks about in the second chapter about the fact that you're complete in him.
point_summary: Paul's goal for the Colossian believers—whom he knew only by report—was their completeness in Christ, the same pastoral desire driving his defense of his conduct before the Corinthians.

context: In whom also ye are circumcised with the circumcision made without hands, and putting off the body of the sins of the flesh by the circumcision of Christ,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: buried with him in baptism, wherein also ye are risen with him through the faith of the operation of God, who hath raised him from the dead.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And you, being dead in your sins and the uncircumcision of your flesh, hath he quickened together with him, having forgiven you all trespasses
point_summary: Dead sinners are made alive together with Christ — "quickened" — and simultaneously granted full forgiveness of all trespasses, as the sermon reads verse 13 to establish before developing the cross imagery of verse 14.

context: blotting out the handwriting of ordinances that was against us, which was contrary to us, and took it out of the way, nailing it to his cross

point_summary: Colossians 2:6 is cited to show that the same Christ received at salvation is the one believers are called to walk in daily, grounding Paul's command to "be perfect" in ongoing, Christ-centered maturity.

context: rooted and built up in him and established in the faith as ye have been taught, abounding therein with thanksgiving
point_summary: Spiritual maturity is not self-generated but grows from being rooted and built up in Christ, established in the faith, and marked by thanksgiving — the very completeness believers already possess in Him.

context: For in Him dwelleth all the fullness of the Godhead bodily
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: and ye are complete in Him, which is the head of all principality and power
point_summary: Completeness belongs to the believer in Christ alone, so that apart from new birth in Him a person remains fundamentally lacking the one thing necessary.

context: we read it in Colossians one, in verse number 14, that we're redeemed
point_summary: Salvation through Christ's blood — redemption and forgiveness of sins — is something money cannot buy, making it the supreme reason for rejoicing above any earthly wealth.

context: He talks about there in Colossians one, the forgiveness of sins that we have that in whom we have redemption through his blood, even the forgiveness of sins
point_summary: Redemption through Christ's blood—not silver or gold—is cited as the supreme possession that money cannot buy, grounding the call to rejoice in salvation above earthly wealth.

context: let the peace of God rule in our hearts
point_summary: Salvation brings a peace of God that money cannot purchase, which believers are called to let rule in their hearts, and which a lost man visibly coveted when he encountered it firsthand.

context: let your speech be always with grace, seasoned with salt that you may know how you ought to answer and respond
point_summary: Speech that is "always with grace, seasoned with salt" leaves no wiggle room for exceptions, and sometimes requires deliberate pause before replying so the response can be properly gracious.

context: salt, that you may know how you ought to answer every man
point_summary: Speech that is "always with grace, seasoned with salt" requires deliberate pause before responding—illustrated by deleting an unconstructive email—so that every reply builds up rather than merely vents the flesh.

context: walk worthy of God
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: Christ in me, the hope of glory
context: Christ in me, the hope of glory
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Notice in Colossians, if you will
point_summary: Risen with Christ, believers are called to redirect their affections upward toward heavenly things rather than remaining fixed on earthly desires that feed the flesh.

context: if ye then be risen with Christ, seek the Lord. those things which are above. Where Christ sitteth on the right hand of God, set your affection on things above, not on things on the earth
point_summary: Because believers have been raised with Christ and are new creatures, they are called to set their affections on things above rather than yielding to the old nature's lusts, which remain a present reality requiring active mortification.

context: for ye are dead, and your life is hid with Christ in God
point_summary: Because the believer is dead and his life is hidden with Christ, the old nature with its lusts belongs to the earth and must be mortified rather than indulged.

context: mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth
point_summary: Believers are commanded to mortify earthly members precisely because the old nature's lusts are real, and denying this self-deceives—echoing James's warning not to err.

context: Look over the book of Colossians, if you will, and look in Colossians chapter 1
point_summary: Paul's love for believers he had never personally met—evident in Colossians—demonstrates that his pastoral concern for edification was not rooted in personal relationship but in apostolic calling.

context: the Apostle Paul simply hears about their faith. He hears from their faithful minister, who is a paphras, according to verse number 7 of the text of Scripture
point_summary: Epaphras, not Paul, personally ministered to the Colossians, yet Paul's pastoral concern extended even to believers he had never met, demonstrating that his love was not contingent on personal relationship.

context: notice what Paul says in Colossians 1 and verse 28, whom we preach, warning every man and teaching every man in all wisdom
point_summary: Paul's pastoral aim—warning, teaching, and presenting every person complete in Christ—extended even to believers he had never personally met, revealing that his labor was driven by love rather than personal advantage.

context: may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus
point_summary: Paul's goal in preaching and teaching every person—whether known to him personally or not—was to present them complete in Christ at the final judgment, which drove his labor and love for all under his sphere of influence.

context: The Bible tells us in Colossians chapter number one and verse number 14, a precious verse of scripture with regard to the subject of being forgiven. It says, in whom we have redemption through his blood, even the forgiveness of sins.
point_summary: Forgiveness of sins is available only through the blood of Christ — redemption and pardon being inseparable from his atoning work — and this is the foundational truth even the youngest believer grasps at salvation.

context: as ye have therefore received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk ye in Him, rooted and built up in Him, established in the faith, as ye have been taught, abounding therein with thanksgiving. Beware lest any man spoil you through philosophy and vain deceit, after the tradition of men
point_summary: Trials serve as God's instrument for developing spiritual maturity because the believer, already complete in Christ positionally, is being progressively brought to that completeness practically.

context: after the rudiments of the world, and not after Christ
point_summary: Colossians 2:8's warning against hollow philosophy frames the surrounding passage on completeness in Christ, which the sermon uses to ground the believer's positional sanctification as the goal trials are working toward.

context: For in Him dwelleth all the fullness of the Godhead bodily
point_summary: Colossians 2:10's declaration that believers are "complete in Him" grounds the sermon's argument that trials serve God's progressive, practical work of bringing that positional completeness to fruition in daily life.

context: and ye are complete in Him, which is the head of all principality and power
point_summary: Believers are already positionally complete in Christ (Col. 2:10), and trials are the means by which God works that standing reality into practical, progressive spiritual maturity.
